frameworks.name: Behavioral vs. Attitudinal Data Hierarchy
frameworks.definition: Behavioral data (what people actually do) is more valuable and revealing than attitudinal data (what people claim they do), because self-reported intentions systematically diverge from revealed preferences.

frameworks.name: Ergodicity Fallacy in Aggregate Statistics
frameworks.definition: The false assumption that what is good on average for a population doing something once is also good for a single individual doing the same thing repeatedly; this misapplication of ensemble averages to time-series decisions produces fatally wrong conclusions in economics, policy, and risk.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that as the volume of data and number of variables increases, the rate at which misleading or false conclusions are generated grows faster than the rate at which true insights are generated, because false correlations and confounding variables multiply faster than valid signals.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: More variables create exponentially more pairwise correlations to test, the majority of which are spurious; simultaneously, confounding variables (unobserved third factors driving apparent correlations) become harder to identify as dataset complexity rises.
bounded_by: Does not imply data is useless; behavioral data remains more valuable than attitudinal data even at scale

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that the primary risk of big data is not the data itself but the absence of a sufficiently questioning and skeptical mindset when interpreting it, and that the first governance question any organization should answer is whether mechanisms exist to prevent catastrophically wrong conclusions from being acted upon.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that behavioral data (records of what people actually do) is more valuable and more revealing than attitudinal data (what people claim they do or intend to do), because observed actions expose preferences that self-reports systematically conceal or distort.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: People's self-reported intentions and behaviors diverge from their revealed actions; attitudinal data captures the self-image a respondent wishes to project, not the causal driver of their behavior.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that one or two unnoticed rogue variables in a dataset are sufficient to render the entire apparent signal meaningless or actively misleading, because the false pattern they create can be indistinguishable from a real one without knowledge of their existence.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: dataset contains gaps or irregular observation periods the analyst is unaware of
claims.mechanism: When observations are missing for non-random reasons (e.g., the fuel card was unused during a France trip), the denominator of a rate calculation is corrupted; the rate appears to change directionally when only the denominator changed, not the underlying phenomenon.

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that averaging across a population loses information rather than gaining it, because the act of aggregation discards variance and second-order factors that may be the most practically important signal in the data.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: When a population is heterogeneous across multiple dimensions, the average value on each dimension does not describe any actual member of that population; the solution designed for the average therefore solves the problem for nobody.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the population is genuinely homogeneous across the relevant dimensions

claims.text: Rory Sutherland asserts that in a sample of 4,000 US Air Force pilots measured across 10 physical dimensions, not a single pilot was average on all 10 dimensions simultaneously, demonstrating that designing for the population average produces a design that fits no individual.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: population measured across multiple independent continuous dimensions
claims.mechanism: Because each physical dimension varies independently, the probability that any individual is near the average on all dimensions simultaneously shrinks exponentially as the number of dimensions grows.

numbers.axis: number of pilots out of 4,000 who are average across all measured dimensions

bounded_by: Applies to multi-dimensional physical or behavioral data; single-dimension averages retain more usefulness

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that in statistical reasoning, even highly educated people with formal credentials can be wrong by a factor of millions in their probability estimates when they misapply statistical concepts, whereas in most other areas of practical mathematics intuition keeps errors within an order of magnitude.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: statistical reasoning under legal or policy pressure
claims.mechanism: Statistical intuitions are not calibrated by everyday experience the way spatial or numerical intuitions are; expert confidence amplifies rather than corrects the error because credentials create social authority that suppresses dissent.

numbers.axis: magnitude of error in expert probability estimates vs. naive intuition

anchors.region: geometric/spatial estimation (e.g., surface area of a cone)
anchors.value: within one order of magnitude (rough heuristic)

bounded_by: Error magnitude varies by the specific conditional probability structure; simpler unconditional probabilities are less affected

claims.text: Rory Sutherland describes the prosecutor's fallacy as the error of computing the probability of a damning prior event given innocence, when the legally correct question is the probability of guilt given the prior event and the observed outcome — and that this inversion can shift apparent probability of guilt from near-zero to greater-than-even odds.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: legal evidence involving prior events and conditional probabilities
claims.mechanism: P(prior event | innocence) and P(guilt | prior event AND current outcome) are different quantities; presenting the first to a jury when the second is the relevant calculation systematically biases verdicts toward conviction when the prior event is rare, and toward acquittal when the prior event seems common.

numbers.axis: probability of spousal guilt when victim had previously reported domestic abuse and is now murdered

anchors.region: prosecutor's fallacy version: P(murdered | domestic abuse complaint)
correct conditional: P(spouse guilty | victim was murdered AND had previously reported domestic abuse)

bounded_by: The magnitude of the inversion depends on the base rates of both the prior event and the charged crime

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that in the Sally Clark double-infanticide case in the UK, Oxford professor Roy Meadows committed multiple compounding statistical errors: multiplying the probability of a single cot death by itself to get a joint probability (ignoring genetic correlation between siblings), failing to adjust for shared environmental factors in the same home, and not comparing the resulting probability against the equally rare probability of double infanticide — transforming what should have been approximately 2-to-1 odds in favor of innocence into a declaration of near-certain guilt.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: joint probability calculation for correlated events in family settings
claims.mechanism: Statistical errors compound: assuming independence between siblings' cot death risk ignores genetic correlation; ignoring shared environment ignores a common-cause variable; omitting the comparison denominator (probability of double infanticide) violates the correct Bayesian framing that requires comparing two competing hypotheses, not a hypothesis against a 1.0 baseline.

numbers.axis: apparent probability of guilt given Roy Meadows' flawed calculation vs. correct Bayesian framing

anchors.region: Roy Meadows' stated odds against double cot death
correct Bayesian odds given competing hypotheses and correlated risk factors
anchors.value: approximately 2 to 1 in favor of innocence

bounded_by: Specific to cases involving joint rare-event probability with correlated causal factors; less relevant to independent-event joint probabilities

claims.text: Rory Sutherland, drawing on the work of Ole Peters at the London Mathematical Laboratory, argues that virtually all of economics rests on a fallacious assumption of ergodicity — that what is optimal on average for a population doing something once is equally optimal for an individual doing the same thing repeatedly — and that this error produces systematically wrong policy and individual decision recommendations.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: decisions involving repeated exposure to the same risk or opportunity
decisions evaluated using ensemble (population) averages rather than time-series outcomes for one individual
claims.mechanism: Ensemble averages smooth over path-dependent outcomes; for a single individual, sequential repetition of a risky action compounds probability of ruin in a way that does not appear in the cross-sectional average, because the dead (ruined) individuals cannot repeat the action and are excluded from the average of survivors.
bounded_by: Ergodicity assumption is valid when outcomes are truly independent and individual outcomes are interchangeable with population samples; fails for absorbing-state risks like ruin or death

claims.text: Rory Sutherland illustrates the ergodicity error with Russian roulette: playing once with a 1-in-6 chance of death has a positive expected monetary value if the payout is large enough, and some individuals will accept it; but playing 6 or 10 times in a row is a near-certain death sentence that almost no payout justifies — demonstrating that a decision rule based on ensemble average value is irrational for a single individual repeating the action.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: sequential repeated exposure to independent risks with absorbing failure state (death or ruin)
claims.mechanism: Each sequential trial with a non-zero death probability compounds; P(surviving N trials) = (5/6)^N, which approaches zero rapidly. The ensemble average (most people survive once) does not predict the time-series outcome for the individual who plays repeatedly.

numbers.axis: survival probability as a function of number of Russian roulette rounds played

anchors.value: 5/6 (~83%) chance of survival; plausible to accept at sufficient payout
(5/6)^6 ~33% survival; almost no payout justifies it

bounded_by: Applies only to decisions with non-recoverable failure states (ruin, death); does not apply when downside is recoverable and the individual can re-enter after a loss

claims.text: Rory Sutherland argues that aggregate measures of overcrowding (average number of people standing per train journey) systematically misidentify the correct problem to solve, because they treat 10 people standing 10% of the time as equivalent to 1 person standing 100% of the time — when only the second case creates severe dissatisfaction and behavioral change.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: service design problems where distribution of experience across users is highly unequal
policy intervention evaluated using aggregate rather than distributional metrics
claims.mechanism: Aggregate averages mask the distribution of harm; infrequent minor inconvenience is mentally filed as acceptable variance, while constant severe inconvenience for a small segment creates committed detractors. Solving the problem for the worst-affected segment eliminates the majority of the net negative sentiment even though it affects a small percentage of total person-journeys.

numbers.axis: standing frequency on public transit and resulting passenger dissatisfaction category

anchors.value: filed mentally as 'shit happens'; no lasting dissatisfaction
severe dissatisfaction; drives churn from the service

bounded_by: Applies to service problems with highly skewed harm distribution; less relevant when harm is uniformly distributed across users

examples.text: Rory Sutherland recounts that roughly 12 years before the time of the talk, Ogilvy issued him a fuel card, and after three years the card provider began reporting his monthly miles-per-gallon. His MPG appeared to decline monotonically every month for eight months, leading him to suspect a fuel leak, theft by neighbors, or a mechanical fault. He eventually realized that before the MPG reporting began, he had taken a trip to France (where the card could not be used) and had also once forgotten to use the card — so the early fill-up intervals recorded were approximately twice the real distance, inflating the apparent baseline MPG. What looked like a long deterioration was entirely a regression to the mean from an artificially high starting point caused by two missing data points. He had nearly accused his neighbors of fuel theft on the strength of this artifact.

Rory Sutherland describes the UK case of Sally Clark, a solicitor convicted of double infanticide in the late 1990s when both her infant sons died, which was almost certainly a double cot death. Oxford professor Roy Meadows testified that the probability of a double cot death was approximately 80 million to one against, obtained by squaring the estimated probability of a single cot death and treating the two events as independent. Meadows failed to account for the genetic correlation between siblings from the same parents (which increases shared risk of cot death), the shared home environment (which would amplify any environmental cause), or the correct Bayesian comparison (which requires comparing the probability of double cot death against the probability of double infanticide, itself an extremely rare event). Sally Clark was convicted, imprisoned, and later exonerated; Professor Meadows was subsequently censured by the Royal Statistical Society.

Rory Sutherland cites the OJ Simpson murder trial as an example of the prosecutor's fallacy operating in reverse (the defender's fallacy). Simpson's defense argued that because spousal abuse complaints lead to murder only approximately 1 in 1,000 times, the prior domestic abuse report against Simpson was nearly irrelevant evidence — implying a guilt probability of only 0.1%. Sutherland argues the correct conditional is: given that Nicole Brown Simpson was already dead and had previously reported domestic abuse, what is the probability that the spouse was responsible? In cases where a murdered person had previously reported domestic abuse, the probability that the spouse was the killer is significantly greater than 50%. The defense exploited the confusion between these two conditional probabilities to neutralize highly probative evidence.

Rory Sutherland describes how the US Air Force's attempt to design a cockpit for the average pilot failed completely. A physical anthropologist hired to define the average pilot measured 10 physical dimensions across 4,000 pilots and found that zero pilots were average on all 10 simultaneously. The anthropologist had previously found the same pattern with human hand measurements: nearly everyone's hand is an outlier on some dimension. The recommended solution was not an average seat but an adjustable seat with adjustable controls, since the average is a solution for nobody when the population is heterogeneous across multiple independent dimensions.

Rory Sutherland proposes train overcrowding as a concrete example of ergodicity-misapplication in policy. Transport planners measure average standing rates per journey and try to solve the aggregate problem. Sutherland argues the correct diagnosis is distributional: most passengers stand occasionally (one journey in ten) and experience it as normal variance; a small subset (commuter season-ticket holders on specific routes) stand on every journey and are intensely dissatisfied. The aggregate metric averages these two populations together and produces an undifferentiated intervention target. His proposed solution — enlarging first-class carriages and permitting annual season-ticket holders to use first class during peak hours on specific commuter routes like Tunbridge Wells to Charing Cross — solves the problem for the intensely dissatisfied minority at low cost without requiring a systemwide fix.

practices.text: Before acting on a data trend, identify whether any gaps in data collection (missed observations, card not used, out-of-region trips) could create a false baseline that makes regression to the mean look like a real deterioration.

When computing joint probabilities for related events (e.g., two deaths in the same family), explicitly test for correlation between the events before treating them as independent; independence is an assumption that must be justified, not a default.

In legal, medical, or policy contexts, always frame probability questions as comparisons between two competing hypotheses (Bayesian framing) rather than computing P(evidence | innocence) in isolation and treating the complement as P(guilt).

When designing a product, seat, policy, or service for a heterogeneous population, replace a fixed-average design with an adjustable or personalized design, because a design optimized for the average satisfies nobody when the population varies across multiple independent dimensions.

Before evaluating a risk or investment decision using expected value, determine whether the decision will be made once (ensemble average is relevant) or repeatedly by the same individual (time-series path dependency is relevant); only the latter requires ergodicity-adjusted analysis.

When diagnosing a service quality problem, segment the affected population by severity of impact rather than reporting only aggregate measures; identify the small subset experiencing the extreme end of the distribution and solve for them first, as this often resolves the majority of net dissatisfaction.
